item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice to see my old SCP number has a better one, I like how the Description talks about all the key points and the Comtaiment procedures make since, nice job 👍

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since when are buildings Euclid?

Tag Search tool using "building" and "euclid" brings up a bunch, but typically it's not the building itself that's the primary anomaly. For cases where it is though, I think it depends on the amount of resources it would take to keep up the cover story, or any additional requirements for keeping people from being affected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since when are buildings Euclid? If it's stationary and doesn't do stuff on its own, it's Safe. A simple cover story suffices.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hadn't read that one when I wrote this. Might make for an interesting crosslink—but, as you know, there is no canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was really, really hoping that the counter was going to tick up one once the Moon came into view.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I included that last sentence as an indirect means of showing the research team's desperation, the growing existential crisis that resulted from what the telescope showed them. They don't want to admit it, they don't want to give up hope, even when all the odds are stacked against them… just like we are in our search for extraterrestrial life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I might go with Euclid (most building SCPs are Euclid), since they have to actively prevent people from going inside and discovering it.

Edit: +1 by the way. This gives me a very nice image of a slow zoom out into space, getting lonelier and lonelier as it goes. Maybe remove the last sentence? That could give it a better vibe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah Safe makes the most sense. Thaumiel is for objects which are critical (or at the least extremely useful) to Foundation operations. Something anomalous that could be useful isn't enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Y'know, the thought crossed my head. But since Thaumiels are still really rare compared to the other object classes, I decided to leave it as Safe because I thought it would draw attention away from the rest of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An interesting Thaumiel candidate perhaps?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: opps

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, the view that 3816 depicts does not show the passage of time.

Oh sorry, can't believe I missed that. Yeah, it doesn't include non-sapient organisms. It's just not possible to estimate the number of organisms that one can see if it did. With this in mind, however, I think the existential horror still manages to persist. It thus begs the question "why are we the only sapient organisms in the universe?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These tests are all presumed to have been conducted in a single "sitting,"

…like, does time stop in the moments that they, do this, though? Even if it were just for a couple of seconds that's enough for the population of, born humans to increase. If we're accounting for them pre-birth I'm pretty sure it's even more, because, humans, and sex.

Also the number does include anomalous organisms.

the number doesn't include non-anomalous, non-sapient organisms

i.e. dogs and cats and mosquitos and stuff. I might've just read it wrong, but even if I did - well, I don't know how big the counter would be. I'm not quite sure if we would be capable of measuring it - obviously though, this telescope would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At this point, SCP-XXXX appears to bypass the Earth itself

hmmmm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you think about it in that way, then when compared to the rest of the universe, wouldn't sapient life itself be the anomaly?

I might be missing something, but I believe I fixed the counter issue by moving the 4/08 test up to 4/09. These tests are all presumed to have been conducted in a single "sitting," so time does not elapse in the field of view.

Also the number does include anomalous organisms. Things like 682, etc. that can be said to be sapient are all counted. It also depends on whether or not you consider extraterrestrial life itself to be anomalous or not, seeing that many SCPs on the site are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I get what you're going for. I think it's not to my personal taste.

My primary issue is that there's one of two premises here: Earth is essentially, mundanely unique throughout the whole of creation - which I, personally, find pretty implausible. The other is that we are anomalously unique throughout the whole of creation, which shifts the dramatic weight of this article from 'we are mundanely alone throughout the whole of creation' to 'we are specially endowed throughout the whole of creation' - which, you know. Not really the same thing, and it's not exactly as existentially frightening, imo.

However I do concede that these gripes are more based within taste and outlook more than the actual quality of the content, so I feel a downvote would probably be in poor faith. I don't think it's a bad article, it just doesn't work on my end.

edit: also, as a followup to ModernMajorGeneral's nit - +-250 children are born every minute. If we're counting children conceived, it's even more than that. I second Laneous in that this essentially undercuts the dramatic device you use of the counter staying static, so it's not an easy issue.

Also… if I'm understanding the criterion correctly, the number doesn't include non-anomalous, non-sapient organisms? Which would potentially leave out extraterrestrials who, are that, thus kind of killing the whole… thematic thrust, or whatever.

edit: After more thought, I've decided to downvote. It's still technically solid, but I have misgivings with the details of the idea itself and the way it was executed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I changed it; I moved the 4/08 test to 4/09.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, I'm sure the Earth's population would have changed over the course of 1 day, unless there is something more to the number remaining static from 4/08 to 4/09 I'm missing.

I was thinking about this too, but I don't really know how the author could show the counter increasing without sorta ruining the main hook of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough. I really tried to go for a minimalist approach with this article, so I can see how some might see it as lacking substance.

That is a valid loophole you have just pointed out to me; thanks for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'Being alone in the universe is scary' is a pretty old philosophical and sci-fi idea. I can understand some people might enjoy this sparse, deadpan presentation, but it didn't really bring anything new to the table.

There are a couple of errors here: 'luminscent' in two of the testing logs and "as of 12:26 PM CST, 4/3/2019" - is this meant to be 4/8/2019 or am I misunderstanding how the object works?

Also, I'm sure the Earth's population would have changed over the course of 1 day, unless there is something more to the number remaining static from 4/08 to 4/09 I'm missing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a world,
Outside my doorstep
Flames over, everyone's heart
Don't you see them shining,
I want to hear them
Beating for me
I think I hear them
Waves crashing me by,
Crashing me by,
Crashing me by

—"Blue Light" by Mazzy Star

Short and sweet. It probably took 15-30 minutes writing the bulk of this thing. And then I let it ferment in my sandbox for a week before I got the energy to post it.

Thanks to:

fenderson, Westrin, AthosNetwork, Uncle Nicolini, Leveritas, and Player 02 for feedback.

Image is from here under CC.
